file_name: Columbia Laboratories, (Bermuda) Ltd. - AMEND NO. 2 TO MANUFACTURING AND SUPPLY AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Columbia shall pay each invoice in full within [***] after the date of receipt by Columbia of such invoice, which shall be issued no earlier than the date on which the Product is delivered to the carrier by Fleet.
perturbation.changed_text: Columbia shall pay each invoice in full within 90 days after the date of receipt by Columbia of such invoice, which shall be issued no earlier than the date on which the Product is delivered to the carrier by Fleet.
perturbation.explanation: The contract originally specifies a payment timeline for invoices as [***]. I'm changing this to 90 days. Depending on the specific goods and the agreement, this could conflict with prompt payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act generally requires federal agencies to pay invoices within 30 days of receipt. While this contract is not directly with a US federal agency, payment terms of 90 days could be considered unreasonable or conflict with standard commercial practices if the transaction falls under regulations influenced by the Prompt Payment Act or similar state-level laws governing payment terms, especially if it involves subcontractors or federally funded projects.

Regulations (a) The Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe regulations to carry out section 3902 of this title . The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment timeline to a fixed 90 days, which contradicts the general requirement of the Prompt Payment Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 31 USC 3903, states regulations to carry out section 3902, which includes the requirement that payment is due under the contract or 30 days after a proper invoice is received. Although the contract is not directly with a federal agency, the change introduces a payment term that potentially conflicts with established payment standards and similar state laws, making the contractual terms unenforceable. The change undermines the legal intent of prompt payment, creating a time-sensitive inconsistency, therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to return to the other party upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement all Confidential Information acquired from such other party, except as to such information it may be required to retain under Applicable Law, and except for one copy of such information to be retained by such party's legal counsel. Neither party shall, during the period of this Agreement nor for five (5) years thereafter, without the other party's express prior written consent, other than as provided under this Agreement, use or disclose any such Confidential Information for any purpose other than to carry out its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to return to the other party upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement all Confidential Information acquired from such other party, except as to such information it may be required to retain under Applicable Law, and except for one copy of such information to be retained by such party's legal counsel. Neither party shall, during the period of this Agreement nor for one (1) year thereafter, without the other party's express prior written consent, other than as provided under this Agreement, use or disclose any such Confidential Information for any purpose other than to carry out its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a 5-year confidentiality clause post-termination. I am changing this to one (1) year. If the confidential information includes trade secrets, this timeframe may be insufficient to protect those trade secrets under applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality clause may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, as the information may still hold value and be protectable under trade secret law beyond that period. The modified clause weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets compared to the indefinite protection offered if the information remains a trade secret. Although Bermuda law is in effect here, the contract is between international businesses and is filed with the SEC, so US law is appropriate here.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces an inconsistency regarding the duration of the confidentiality clause, potentially contradicting trade secret law. The original contract stipulates a 5-year confidentiality period post-termination, while the changed text reduces this to one year. The Perturbation Explanation says that if the confidential information includes trade secrets, this timeframe may be insufficient to protect those trade secrets under applicable law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating the UTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality clause may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, as the information may still hold value and be protectable under trade secret law beyond that period. The changed text weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets compared to the indefinite protection offered if the information remains a trade secret. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because the 'time-sensitive obligation' in the contract (the length of the confidentiality clause) does not adequately protect trade secrets as required by law, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Columbia QA, any other person appointed by Columbia, Columbia's customer, and/or any Regulatory Authority may conduct inspections and audits of Fleet's manufacturing facility, Columbia Equipment, quality control laboratories, and other quality systems relating to the manufacture and storage of the Product according to Columbia's reasonable procedures upon reasonable prior written notice, during normal business hours, provided, however, that Columbia QA, any other person appointed by Columbia and/or any Regulatory Authority may conduct a "For Cause" audit during normal business hours upon three (3) business days prior written notice to Fleet.
perturbation.changed_text: Columbia QA, any other person appointed by Columbia, Columbia's customer, and/or any Regulatory Authority may conduct inspections and audits of Fleet's manufacturing facility, Columbia Equipment, quality control laboratories, and other quality systems relating to the manufacture and storage of the Product according to Columbia's procedures with no prior written notice, during normal business hours, provided, however, that Columbia QA, any other person appointed by Columbia and/or any Regulatory Authority may conduct a "For Cause" audit during normal business hours upon one (1) business day prior written notice to Fleet.
perturbation.explanation: I removed the 'reasonable prior written notice' for standard audits and changed 'three' to 'one' business day written notice for 'For Cause' audits, potentially conflicting with regulations on pharmaceutical manufacturing oversight.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.22 mandates a quality control unit with responsibilities that include approving or rejecting all procedures or specifications impacting product quality. While the regulation doesn't explicitly state the notice period for audits, eliminating 'reasonable prior written notice' for routine audits and reducing it to one day for 'For Cause' audits may hinder Fleet's ability to adequately prepare for and address potential issues raised during the audit. This lack of preparation time could undermine the effectiveness of the audit process, potentially violating the spirit and intent of thorough quality oversight as required by 21 CFR § 211.22. The change impacts quality control. The UK facility still manufactures drugs for US distribution, so the US regulation applies.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the law by reducing the notice period for audits. The original text requires 'reasonable prior written notice' for standard audits, which is removed entirely in the changed text, and reduces the notice for 'For Cause' audits from three days to one day. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 211.22 mandates thorough quality oversight. By removing 'reasonable prior written notice', the changed text hinders Fleet's ability to adequately prepare for audits and address potential issues, which could undermine the effectiveness of the audit process. The scraped snippets support the relevance of 21 CFR to the contract. This creates a direct conflict with the intent of the regulation. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because a time-sensitive obligation (the notice period) in the contract does not align with legal requirements, since it undermines quality control measures.
